title: Extracting alignment data in open models
abstract:
In this work, we show that it is possible to extract significant amounts of alignment training data from a post-trained model -- useful to steer the model to improve certain capabilities such as long-context reasoning, safety, instruction following, and maths. 
While the majority of related work on memorisation has focused on measuring success of training data extraction through string matching, we argue that embedding models are better suited for our specific goals. 
Distances measured through a high quality embedding model can identify semantic similarities between strings that a different metric such as edit distance will struggle to capture. 
In fact, in our investigation, approximate string matching would have severely undercounted (by a conservative estimate of $10\times$) the amount of data that can be extracted due to trivial artifacts that deflate the metric. Interestingly, we find that models readily regurgitate training data that was used in post-training phases such as SFT or RL. We show that this data can be then used to train a base model, recovering a meaningful amount of the original performance. 
We believe our work exposes a possibly overlooked risk towards extracting alignment data. Finally, our work opens up an interesting discussion on the downstream effects of distillation practices: since models seem to be regurgitating aspects of their training set, distillation can therefore be thought of as indirectly training on the model's original dataset.
decision_comment: This paper makes an interesting observation: recent methods that ask LLMs to generate useful synthetic data with templates can be used to get models to reveal (regurgitate) their post-training data. To demonstrate this, they show that it is important to look at semantic similarity between generated strings and training data strings. These observations have implications and add to the already extensive literature on memorization and pre-/post-training data inference attacks. The work is well-executed overall, but the contributions/impact are likely to be incremental/marginal (because of the data needed to carry out the regurgitation attack).

reviews.summary: This paper attempts to demonstrate a process by which alignment data (e.g. data used in SFT or RLVR portions of training) can be readily extracted from a trained (open) model. While it is known that large models often memorize and regurgitate training data, the authors find that surprisingly sophisticated elements of post-training (such as RL prompts and traces) can also be extracted. The authors also demonstrate the importance of appropriate distance metrics (i.e semantic similarity in an embedding space) for accurately capturing the amount of data that can be extracted from a trained model (previous string distances cause underestimation). The methods are limited to open models that make their post-training data mixtures available (since it utilizes chat templates). The authors are able to extract enough alignment data to train a distilled model to a high fraction of original model performance.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper has a moderate degree of technical soundness. Utilizing semantic similarity as a proxy for memorization has inherent ambiguities, ones the authors try to address by demonstrating several example comparisons and appealing to reasonable human judgement. Given the paper places a strong focus on contextualizing the value of extracted data under its usefulness in training distilled models, this ambiguity is somewhat addressed. Authors are careful not to overstate the applicability of their work, and experiments are appropriate to validate their posed hypotheses. 

The paper has a moderate degree of presentation quality. Concepts are well-defined, all technical terms are either standard terminology or appropriately defined, existing literature and unique contributions are well discriminated, and diagrams help elucidate key concepts. 

The paper has a low to moderate degree of significance. The key insight (that alignment data can be extracted from certain classes of models) is very important in the context of preventing unauthorized model distillation (where models are trained using proprietary data and thus need to preserve a competitive advantage); however, in many of the areas the authors discuss (such as privacy), the applicability of embedding distance is hard to quantify. Extracted data may be semantically similar while obfuscating key values (such as dates or coordinates). Furthermore, only open models with exposed chat templates are vulnerable to this kind of attack, making its applicability limited to being able to train similarly performing models using extracted data with high semantic similarity. The path to applying this method to frontier closed models trained on proprietary data is not straightforward. 

The paper has a low to moderate degree of originality. While it may be surprising that alignment data can be extracted with sufficient quality and volume to train models to comparable performance, one of the main contribution (utilizing semantic distance instead of string matching distance) is generally a well utilized practice. The usage of string distances in prior work is likely driven by the strict adherence to memorization as a rote duplication of training and alignment data. The prompting strategy utilized to extract the alignment data differs enough from prior work as to constitute an original contribution.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
(1) Do the authors intend to do controlled experiments on whether high semantic distances correlate with specific identifiable details in alignment data? While the extracted anecdotes demonstrate this, it is difficult to extrapolate why instances where low string distance matching values but high semantic similarity values would be more likely caused by changes in "style" and not changes to quantitative content of alignment data. For example, in figure 12 the initial height is changed from 55 to 54 inches. There is probably some connection to be made here with the entropy-memorisation law. Strengthening this finding would have important implications in the use of semantic distance for privacy applications and improve overall significance. 

(2) Have the authors attempted this attack on closed models by enumerating common chat templates? Geng et al. 2005 has some limited discussion, but more evidence of this being a potential attack vector would help both the significance and originality of this paper.

(3) The authors  note that they "develop a more subtle notion of training data extraction where the semantic structure is just as valuable as the literal content" -- are there examples of this scenario in the extracted alignment data? Many of the cited examples in the main paper and appendix rely on specific details rather than unique semantic structure. Additional examples would help significance of the paper.

reviews.summary: This work presents a method that can extract alignment data (including SFT and RL data) from LLMs. The extraction attack leverages chat templates, specifically, the special tokens introduced during post-training. It utilizes the special token to prompt models into regurgitating their training data. The authors demonstrate their method on OLMo 2 (sft data extraction) and Open-Reasoner-Zero (RL data extraction) models, showing that extracted data is insightful. The paper also highlights the counter-intuitive finding that RL training samples, including reasoning traces, can be regurgitated.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

The proposed method is highly creative and intuitive. Leveraging the chat template are unique to the post-training phase is a clever idea. 

Weaknesses:

While the overall methodology is insightful, there are significant concerns regarding the quality and actual coverage of the extracted instruction data. The results in Figure 6 show that while the CDF distribution approaches 100% coverage at a similarity threshold of around 0.75, the authors themselves argue that a much higher embedding similarity (e.g., 0.95) is required to consider the data as effectively "memorized" or "extracted" from the post-training set. At this 0.95 threshold, the coverage drops to below 10%. The histograms further confirm that the proportion of generated data with high similarity is actually quite low. This casts doubt on the efficiency of the attack and the completeness of the dataset that can be practically recovered.

The experiments utilizing the extracted data for training, as well as the variety of models probed, are somewhat limited. While the choice of OLMo is well-justified due to its transparency and the availability of its training data, the study would be strengthened by including other settings. For example, using the Qwen/LLaMA base model paired with open-source SFT datasets (such as the Nemotron post-training series) would provide a robust alternative setting for research. While the RL data section (Section 4) employs a similar comparative approach, the current experimental settings generally rely on a single model for each case (SFT or RL), which feels insufficient to draw broad generalizations about the vulnerability of all open models.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors: Regarding the distillation experiment in Section 3.3, could you clarify how the CoT were obtained? Did the model generate the query and then immediately answer it with a CoT in the same generation pass? Or did you use the proposed method solely to extract the instructions/queries, and then perform a separate rollout and rejection sampling process to generate the responses?

reviews.summary: This paper investigates whether alignment data used during post-training can be extracted from open-weight language models. The authors show that prompting models with chat-template tokens can induce generations that closely resemble samples from their SFT or RL training data, and argue that semantic embedding similarity provides a more appropriate measure of extraction than approximate string matching. Using this pipeline, they identify semantically similar training examples and demonstrate that synthetic datasets constructed from these generations can recover a meaningful fraction of the original model’s performance when used for training. The results suggest that distillation may implicitly recover aspects of a model’s original alignment data.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
**Strengths**
1. **The paper studies an important and timely question with clear practical relevance.** The question of whether open models leak useful post-training or alignment data, and whether current evaluations underestimate this phenomenon, is practically important for open-weight releases and model distillation. The work highlights that semantic similarity based extraction may reveal substantially more memorization than previously detected through approximate string matching. This framing is relevant for discussions around open model policies and alignment data reuse.
2. **Simple and intuitive extraction pipeline.** The proposed approach of prompting models with chat template tokens and measuring semantic similarity provides a clear and accessible method for studying alignment data extraction. The paper also presents examples where embedding similarity identifies semantically equivalent samples that would be missed by string based matching. These examples help motivate why semantic similarity is a more appropriate metric for the type of extraction studied in this work.
3. **Useful downstream validation through synthetic data training.** The paper goes beyond retrieval analysis by showing that synthetic datasets constructed from extracted samples can be used to train another model and recover a meaningful portion of the original model’s performance. This strengthens the practical relevance of the findings by demonstrating that the extracted data can transfer useful capabilities rather than simply producing similar outputs.

**Weaknesses**
1. **Limited experimental coverage across models and post-training setups.** The empirical evaluation focuses on a relatively small number of models and training pipelines. While these models provide useful case studies, it is unclear how broadly the results generalize across other open models, architectures, or alignment pipelines. Additional experiments across more models or training configurations would help strengthen the generality of the conclusions.
2. **Sensitivity to prompting and sampling choices is not fully explored.** The extraction pipeline relies on specific prompting strategies and sampling hyperparameters. Sampling strategies such as temperature or nucleus sampling can significantly affect generation behavior. It would be helpful to understand how robust the extraction results are to different decoding configurations.
3. **Interpretation of semantic similarity based extraction could benefit from additional validation.** The paper relies on embedding similarity to detect extracted samples. This is a reasonable approach but it may introduce false positives when applied at scale. In related data decontamination work, embedding similarity is often followed by additional filtering or verification stages such as LLM-based evaluation or manual validation. Additional analysis on the data distributions and useful domains/metadata could point to further learnings.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. The experiments appear to use a fixed sampling configuration such as temperature = 1. How sensitive are the extraction results to other decoding strategies such as nucleus sampling or different temperature values?
2. The experiments focus on a relatively small set of models and post-training pipelines. Do the authors expect similar extraction behavior across other open models or alignment procedures? For example, RL'ing with Qwen models show interesting behaviors (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.20571), so cross-model analysis is useful signal.
3. How does the distribution of the generated dataset compare to the original alignment dataset, and how sensitive is downstream performance to filtering or rebalancing strategies?
4. How much of a dataset can we "recover"? Using the OLMo post-training dataset, can we recover a large percentage or approximate the scaling curve of the synthetic data?

reviews.summary: This paper demonstrates a risk associated with open models: the authors observed that introducing chat templates and their special tokens during the post-training (SFT/RL) phase, and repeatedly sampling using these template prefixes, can induce the model to generate samples that are highly similar to, or even identical to, the 'aligned' data distribution. The authors propose using 'gemini-embedding-001' to perform vector retrieval between the generated samples and the publicly available post-training dataset, using an embedding similarity threshold (0.95) as the criterion for "approximate semantic memory," replacing the original method of string matching (edit distance). The experimental section covers two lines: on the OLMo2 13B SFT dataset, the authors demonstrate the difference between string matching and embedding retrieval; furthermore, on RL data, the authors show that using template prefix sampling can retell the text in the training set and express some thought processes, which contradicts the intuition of reinforcement learning. Finally, the paper connects these phenomena to distillation practices, pointing out that "model distillation is as data distillation," and obtaining from its alignment data is therefore at risk of being leaked.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
Intuitive method, low implementation threshold: Utilizing chat templates as an attack (prefix) is concise and highly consistent with the introduction stage of template tokens in practice; prefix ablation experiments also support the claim that "templates do indeed guide the distribution."

Concrete comparison: Demonstrates the failure of string metrics such as Levenshtein in "semantic approximation" scenarios through concrete examples, and demonstrates the weakness of string similarity with detailed examples in the appendix

Comprehensive coverage of both SFT and RL post-training paradigms: Demonstrates word-for-word paraphrasing (Q-A pairs) and the phenomenon of "likelihood increase" in RL scenarios, providing insights into the relationship between RL and memory.

Weaknesses:
1. Lack of cost analysis. Using string edit distance may simply be an economical idea.

2. Some phenomena observed in RL data are interesting, but there are no explanations or speculations yet.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1.If only data distillation is needed in the engineering process, does that mean that the retrieval process is unnecessary, and therefore there is no difference from the previous method (derived from the prefix)?

2. If the standard is lowered to <0.95 (i.e., accepting more aligned data), will the performance in Table 2 improve?

3. How to understand Figure 6(a)? My understanding is that only the training samples with the red background have the potential to become Extracted Data. As a result, is it possible to reproduce good post-training performance with only this small subset? (i.e., these are some important "template sets")

4. According to Table 2, using Extracted Data can achieve better performance than using the original training data. However, it has been shown that semantically similar aligned data can be reproduced. So, will this work for **non-public** post-training data datasets?
